old_claim.note: Humanism provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Petrarch and Boccaccio tied early Renaissance humanism to vernacular literary prestige, raising vernacular literature toward the status of classical antiquity.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction: Britannica says Boccaccio and Petrarch laid foundations for Renaissance humanism and raised vernacular literature to the level and status of the classics of antiquity.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source directly connects Petrarch and Boccaccio's humanism with elevated vernacular literary status.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports retaining humanism as an enabling intellectual context for the Renaissance vernacular literature node while keeping earlier vernacular traditions as separate historical lineage.

ontology_before: The humanism edge was an unsourced generic enabling dependency.
ontology_after: The humanism edge is source-backed to Petrarch and Boccaccio's role in joining Renaissance humanism with vernacular literary prestige.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: humanism remains an enabling prerequisite of vernacular_literature_prose_poetry.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to medieval troubadour or Sicilian-school vernacular literature before Renaissance humanism.
A stronger source showed Petrarch and Boccaccio's humanism was unrelated to vernacular literary status.
The humanism edge were promoted to a required dependency rather than a contextual enabling edge.
short_reason: Petrarch and Boccaccio connect Renaissance humanism to vernacular literary prestige.
